coredata.dc:title: Solid-state N,P-doped carbon dots conquer aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching and couple with europium metal-organic frameworks toward white light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Sujie
Chen, Xipao
Hu, Yaoping
Han, Lei
coredata.dc:description: As a newly discovered photoluminescent material, carbon dots (CDs) have shown promising performance in many fields. However, due to aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching, most of CDs are poorly emissive in the solid state, which severely restricts their wide applications, especially in the optoelectronic devices. To overcome this problem, herein, we design a novel kind of self-quenching-resistant N,P-doped CDs, using polyethyleneimine (PEI), phosphoric acid, and ethanol as raw ingredients. The as-synthesized CDs are amorphous and contain abundant surface PEI chains which properly separate the individual carbon core and prevent the direct π–π interaction. Such a unique structure conquers self-quenching during aggregation, and gives CDs a bright blue fluorescence in solid state without any other additional matrices. By taking advantage of their excellent optical properties, the CD powers, combined with red emissive europium metal-organic frameworks, are successfully employed as hybrid phosphors for construction of white light-emitting diodes exhibiting a Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinate of (0.33, 0.33).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-state fluorescence
N,P-doped carbon dots
Europium metal-organic frameworks
Hybrid phosphors
White light-emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CD@Eu-MOF hybrid phosphors for WLED application

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of N,P-doped CDs, (c) XRD pattern and (d) FTIR spectrum of N,P-doped CDs. Inset in (a): size distribution of N,P-doped CDs.
(a) XPS full scan, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) P2p spectra of N,P-doped CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the aqueous CD solution and powder. (b) and (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of the aqueous CD solution and powder. (d) Fluorescence decay curves of the aqueous CD solution and powder (excitation 365 nm, emission 465 nm). Insets in (b) and (c): photographs of the aqueous CD solution and powder under daylight and UV light.

(a) XRD patterns of the as-synthesized and simulated Eu-MOF. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of Eu-MOF. Insets are the photographs of the Eu-MOF powder under daylight and UV light. (c) Schematic view of the WLED using the CD/Eu-MOF hybrid phosphors, and photograph of the working WLED. (d) Emission spectrum of the working WLED. Inset is the corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram.

head.ce:title: Solid-state N,P-doped carbon dots conquer aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching and couple with europium metal-organic frameworks toward white light-emitting diodes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a newly discovered photoluminescent material, carbon dots (CDs) have shown promising performance in many fields. However, due to aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching, most of CDs are poorly emissive in the solid state, which severely restricts their wide applications, especially in the optoelectronic devices. To overcome this problem, herein, we design a novel kind of self-quenching-resistant N,P-doped CDs, using polyethyleneimine (PEI), phosphoric acid, and ethanol as raw ingredients. The as-synthesized CDs are amorphous and contain abundant surface PEI chains which properly separate the individual carbon core and prevent the direct π–π interaction. Such a unique structure conquers self-quenching during aggregation, and gives CDs a bright blue fluorescence in solid state without any other additional matrices. By taking advantage of their excellent optical properties, the CD powers, combined with red emissive europium metal-organic frameworks, are successfully employed as hybrid phosphors for construction of white light-emitting diodes exhibiting a Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinate of (0.33, 0.33).

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention due to outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, cost-effective preparation, and easy functionalization [1–3]. These great advantages make CDs promising candidates for use in sensing, bioimaging, catalysis, energy storage, optoelectronic devices, and so on [4–11]. However, CDs commonly suffer from aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching in the solid state, ascribed to the direct π–π interaction or excessive resonance energy transfer [12], which prevents their practical applications in light-emitting devices. To avoid this undesirable effect, embedding CDs into solid matrices such as polymers [13–18], silica [19–21], layered double hydroxides [22], boron oxynitride [23], and inorganic salts [24–28], have been measured to retain the fluorescence. As a matter of fact, the roles of solid matrices are like water, where CDs can homogeneously disperse. In these cases, the fluorescence quantum yields of the CD@matrix composites achieve high values when only a small amount of CDs load, but decrease dramatically at high CD loading fraction. That is to say, these have not yet overcome self-quenching induced by aggregation. Hence, efforts are needed to explore self-quenching-resistant CDs. Very recently, Chen et al. prepared CDs with intense yellow-green fluorescence in the aggregation state by using poly (vinyl alcohol) and ethylenediamine as precursors [12]. Qu group introduced a space-confined vacuum heating method to produce CDs emitting equally efficient fluorescence in both dispersed and aggregated states [29]. Despite a few impressive reports, the research on the synthesis of CDs conquering aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching is still in an early stage.

In this work, we design a solvothermal method using polyethyleneimine (PEI), phosphoric acid, and ethanol as raw ingredients to prepare novel N,P-doped CDs with strong solid-state fluorescence. The CDs are surrounded by a large amount of branched PEI chains, which provide prominent steric hindrance to prevent close contact of carbon cores and avoid the direct π–π interaction between inner particles. Benefiting from this factor, CDs overcome fluorescence quenching during aggregation. For practical application, the CD powders are simply mixed with europium metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs) to serve as color-converting phosphors in white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs).

Polyethyleneimine (PEI, molecular weight 600), phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85%), europium nitrate hexahydrate [Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, molecular weight 446.06], pyridine-3,5-dicarboxylic acid (H2pdc, molecular weight 167.12), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Aladdin. Nitric acid (HNO3, 98%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37.5%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and ethanol (EtOH, 95%) were got from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received, without any further purification or distillation.

In a typical synthesis, 1 g of PEI, 8 mL of H3PO4, and 16 mL of ethanol were mixed in a 50-mL beaker. Then, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h to produce N,P-doped CDs. After reaction, the pH of the produced solution was adjusted to about 7 by NaOH. Next, the CD dispersion was filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane, followed by purifying in a 1000-Da dialysis tube for 72 h. Finally, about 1 g of light-yellow CD powder was collected by evaporating the solvent and vacuum drying at 60 °C for one night.

The red emissive Eu-MOFs were synthesized according to the previous work with some modification [30]. Briefly, 0.280 g of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and 0.154 g of H2pdc were dissolved in 50 mL of DMF, and heated in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 115 °C for 24 h. After reaction, a yellow needle-shaped Eu-MOF powder was obtained.

The N,P-doped CD powders were homogeneously mixed with Eu-MOFs in a mass ratio of 4:1 to serve as color-converting phosphors. WLED was fabricated by coating the CD@Eu-MOF phosphors on an ultraviolet emissive (365 nm) InGaN chip using the AB glue as a cementing agent curing at 60 °C for 2 h. The working voltage and current of the LED lamp were set at 4 V and 150 mA, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by the JEM-2100 electron microscope at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data was collected on the Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Ká radiation (1.54056 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained on the Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer using the KBr medium. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was got from the AXIS ULTRA DLD spectrograph with Al/Ka as the source. Absorption spectra of the CD solution and powder were obtained on the UV-3101PC spectrophotometer and PERSEE T10CS UV–vis spectrophotometer, respectively. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on the Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were taken on the Horiba FL3-111 spectrometer. Absolute quantum yields were tested using the QE-2100 quantum efficiency measurement system.

TEM and XRD were employed to characterize the morphology and structure of CDs. The TEM image in Fig. 1 a shows that CDs are nearly spherical without apparent aggregation. Their diameters mainly distribute in the range of 2–6 nm with a mean value of about 4 nm (inset of Fig. 1a). As observed in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1b), CDs, without obvious lattice fringe, have an amorphous nature, which demonstrates that the precursor of PEI is not completely graphitized, and a part of branched PEI chains serve as surface groups covering the noncrystalline CD core. In the XRD pattern (Fig. 1c), a broad diffraction peak is centered at 22.0°, far away from the graphite's 002 reflection (26.5°), indicating the poor crystallization of CDs [31]. This result is consistent with the analysis of the HRTEM image. FTIR and XPS were measured to identify the bonding composition and functional groups of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1d, the predominant and broad stretching vibration at 3421 cm−1 demonstrate the presence of a large amount of –NH2/–OH. The absorption peaks at 1643 and 1528 cm−1 are assigned to the CC/CN stretching. The strong band at 1079 cm−1 is attributed to the C–N/C–O/P–O bonding [32]. Other two characteristic peaks at 2869 and 1462 cm−1 confirm the existence of C–H [33]. These FTIR interpretations prove the formation of aromatic constituents and the remaining of PEI fragments in CDs. The XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. 2 a) displays five distinct signals of O1s, N1s, C1s, P2s, and P2p at 532, 400, 284, 190, and 134 eV, respectively, revealing that CDs are primarily composed of C, N, O, and P elements. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) can be fitted into four peaks at 284.5, 285.4, 286.0 and 288.1 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–N/C–P, C–O, and CO/CN groups, respectively [34,35]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) shows the presence of C–N–C at 399.2 eV and N–H at 401.1 eV [36,37]. In the P2p spectrum (Fig. 2d), two peaks at 133.7 and 134.6 eV are associated with the species of P–O and P–C/PO, respectively [38,39]. To summarize all the characterization data, the as-prepared material is a N,P-doped carbonaceous nanodot, consisting of an amorphous core surrounded by plentiful functional groups including PEI chains, hydroxyl, and phosphate.

The absorption spectrum of aqueous CD solution (Fig. 3 a) shows two representative bands at 210 and 361 nm, related with the π–π* transitions of aromatic constituents and n–π* transitions of O/N-containing groups, respectively [40]. The CD powder exhibits the red-shift of absorption bands to 265 and 389 nm. As shown in Fig. 3b, the aqueous CD solution has an excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior with a main emission band around 469–519 nm under the excitation of 340–460 nm. CDs can also disperse in DMSO and acetone, and show the solvent-dependent emission (Fig. S1). It seems that smaller solvent polarity (solvent polarity, H2O > DMSO > acetone) leads to blue-shift of photoluminescence. As usual among most reported fluorescent nanocarbons, the aqueous CD solution is blue emissive under UV light. Uncommonly, the solid-state CD powder retains the characteristic fluorescence (Fig. 3c), demonstrating the effective conquering of aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence decay curves of the aqueous CD solution and powder almost overlap with each other. Both of them fit double-exponential functions (Table S1), giving large proportions of short lifetimes (both of τ1, ∼7 ns, ≥ 96.0%) and small fractions of long lifetimes (both of τ2, >22 ns, ≤ 4.0%). This result indicates that two emissive centers, i.e., inner conjugated π-domains and outer surface defects, exist [41], and the former one from carbon core make the dominant contribution to the emission. The nearly unchanged fluorescence lifetime of the CD powder compared to that of the aqueous CD solution also verifies the suppression of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) in the solid state, because FRET competes with radiative transition and shortens the lifetime [42]. The absolute quantum yields of the aqueous CD solution and powder are identical, exhibiting the value of 4.4%, which further suggests the complete overcoming of self-quenching during aggregation.

It is believed that the solid-emissive property of CDs has a close relationship with the structure [12]. Abundant branched PEI chains on the surface offer sufficient steric hindrance to prevent the direct contact of carbon cores with each other. So, in the aggregation state, CDs are separated individually and keep appropriate intervals (beyond the Förster distance) between the inner particles. In such a condition, the π-π interaction of carbon cores, as well as the FRET process, are efficiently avoided. As a result, CDs conquer aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD@Eu-MOF hybrid phosphors for WLED application
ce:section.ce:para: Benefiting from their unique solid-state fluorescence, the CD powder has a potential application in WLED. The commercial LED emits UV light (Fig. S2). In order to convert the UV light into white light, the single use of blue emissive CD powder as a phosphor is unfeasible. Further incorporation of a red emissive material may be helpful, and the luminescent Eu-MOF is ideal because of its facile synthesis [30]. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, the XRD pattern of the as-synthesized Eu-MOF matches well with the simulated profile, demonstrating its successful preparation. Eu-MOF possesses three emission peaks at around 596, 620, and 705 nm (Fig. 4b), which is originated from the 5D0→7F1, 5D0→7F2.and 5D0→7F4 transitions of the Eu3+ ion, respectively. The quantum yield of Eu-MOF is measured to be 4.0%. Under a UV lamp irradiation, intense red fluorescence of Eu-MOF is observed. We mixed the CD powder with Eu-MOF in a mass ratio of 4:1 to make a white-light-converting phosphor. The CD@Eu-MOF mixture shows the combined fluorescence feature from CDs and Eu-MOF (Fig. S3), possessing a quantum yield of 2.7%. After coating the CD@Eu-MOF on the UV chip, a WLED with a CIE coordinate of (0.33, 0.33) has been fabricated, which performs a white light with three emission components from UV chip, CDs, and Eu-MOF (Fig. 4c&d). The correlated color temperature (CCT) and color rendering index (CRI) values of the white light are 3319.6 K and 76.3, respectively. The two values closely meet the requirements of warm WLEDs for indoor lighting applications (a low CCT < 4000 K, a high CRI > 80) [1].

In conclusion, N,P-doped CDs with blue solid state fluorescence are synthesized by a one-pot solvothermal method. Abundant branched PEI chains cover the surface of CDs, preventing the amorphous cores from direct π-π interaction and avoiding the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching. By simply mixing the CD powder with red emissive Eu-MOF, a hybrid CD@Eu-MOF phosphor is fabricated for WLED application. This self-quenching-resistant CD material may create more opportunity for practical usage in high-performance optoelectronic devices and beyond.

Sujie Han: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Xipao Chen: Investigation. Yaoping Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Lei Han: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2004), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2019A610188), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.109090.
